title: practical english usage - Part 144
content:
She bought six. (     .. .six-ones?) not I'll take both, (     ... both: ones?) not (     ... I-bought-some-ones-today) 'Are there any grapes? 'Yes, I bought some today.' not Take your coat and pass me mine, (     .. . pass me my one.) or a number.

We   do not use one(s) immediately after my, your, etc, some, several, (a) few, both 6 not   used    after    my,    etc,   some,      several,        a  few,     both      or  a  number I don't think much  of these. (More natural than ... these ones.) We   nearly always leave out ones after these and those.

The  adjectives ill (►  494) and well (►  622) are most common after verbs. Before a floating leaf - The ship's still afloat.

alike, alive, alone, asleep, awake. Compare: linking verbs, especially be.
